coredata.dc:title: One step synthesis of hydrophobic carbon dots powder with solid state emission and application in rapid visualization of latent fingerprints
coredata.dc:creator: Bandi, Rajkumar
Kannikanti, Harsha gavash
dadigala, Ramakrishna
Gangapuram, Bhagavanth reddy
Vaidya, Jayathirtha rao
Guttena, Veerabhadram
coredata.dc:description: A one step thermal carbonization method for the preparation of solid state emitting N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) powder with hydrophobic nature is reported here. This method directly offers glowing NCDs powder in large quantities and do not involve tedious purification and drying steps. Physicochemical properties of as produced NCDs were explored using various analytical techniques and optical properties were studied in different solvents. The NCDs powder was applied for the rapid visualization of latent fingerprints (LFPs) on various porous and non-porous surfaces. With their green emission, NCDs were able to provide sufficient contrast on auto-fluorescent surfaces also. The LFPs developed with NCDs powder clearly presented level 2 and level 3 ridge details which are sufficient for the individual identification.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Organophilic carbon dots
Aggregation induced quenching
Solid state synthesis
Fingerprints detection
Powder dusting

The authors would like to thank DST-FIST, New Delhi, India for providing necessary analytical facilities in the department.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FT-IR spectra of reactants and product (NCDs) (b) XPS survey spectra (c) high resolution C 1s spectra and (d) high resolution N 1s spectra of NCDs.
(a) TEM image (b) Particle size distribution (c) XRD pattern and (d) Raman spectrum of NCDs.
(a) Excitation and emission spectra NCDs powder (b) emission spectra of NCDs powder at various excitation wavelengths.
(a) UV–vis absorption, excitation and emission of NCDs dispersed in ethanol (b) corresponding emission spectra at various excitation wavelengths.
(a) Photostability and (b) storage stability of NCDs in solid state and in ethanol.
Absorption, excitation and emission spectra of NCDs in (a) Methanol (b) DMSO (c) Ethyl acetate and (d) Toluene.

Fluorescent image of NCDs developed fingerprint (under 365 nm UV light) showing (1) primary (2) secondary-minutiae (a) Short ridge (b) Bifurcation (c) Ridge ending (d) Lake (e) Double bifurcation (f) hook/spur (g) Core (h) Delta and (3) tertiary level details-Sweat pores.

Fluorescent images of LFPs on different substrate surfaces under 365 nm UV light: (a) Compact disc, (b) Glass slide, (c) Auto fluorescent lid, (d) Magazine paper, (e) leather keychain, (f) Floppy disk, (g) Aluminium surface and (h) Journal cover page.
Probable formation mechanism of NCDs powder.
Optical properties of NCDs in various solvents.

head.ce:title: One step synthesis of hydrophobic carbon dots powder with solid state emission and application in rapid visualization of latent fingerprints

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Osmania University, Hyderabad, Telangana, 500007, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Osmania University, Hyderabad, Telangana, 500007, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Forest Science, College of Forest and Environmental Sciences, Kangwon National University, Chuncheon, 24341, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Forest and Environmental Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Forest Science, College of Forest and Environmental Sciences, Kangwon National University, Chuncheon, 24341, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fluoro Agro Chemicals Division and AcSIR-Indian Institute of Chemical Technology, Uppal Road Tarnaka, Hyderabad, 500007, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fluoro Agro Chemicals Division and AcSIR-Indian Institute of Chemical Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Fluoro Agro Chemicals Division and AcSIR-Indian Institute of Chemical Technology, Uppal Road Tarnaka, Hyderabad, 500007, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Palamuru University, Mahabub Nagar, Telangana, 509001, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Palamuru University, Mahabub Nagar, Telangana, 509001, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A one step thermal carbonization method for the preparation of solid state emitting N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) powder with hydrophobic nature is reported here. This method directly offers glowing NCDs powder in large quantities and do not involve tedious purification and drying steps. Physicochemical properties of as produced NCDs were explored using various analytical techniques and optical properties were studied in different solvents. The NCDs powder was applied for the rapid visualization of latent fingerprints (LFPs) on various porous and non-porous surfaces. With their green emission, NCDs were able to provide sufficient contrast on auto-fluorescent surfaces also. The LFPs developed with NCDs powder clearly presented level 2 and level 3 ridge details which are sufficient for the individual identification.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a newly emerging star in fluorescent nanomaterials gained substantial attention from the scientific community since their serendipitous discovery [1]. With their alluring properties such as admirable chemical & photo stability, unique optical properties, excellent biocompatibility and high water solubility, CDs were advocated for diversified applications [2–6]. Furthermore, these unique features of CDs made them as future alternatives for conventional fluorescent dyes and semiconductor quantum dots [7,8].

However, the CDs still suffer from various drawbacks that can limit the applicability of their unique properties to wide range of fields. Like many organic dyes, the fluorescence of CDs is also severely quenched in the solid state due to aggregation caused quenching effect (ACQ) that might be a consequence of excessive resonance energy transfer (RET) or direct pi – pi interactions [9,10]. Furthermore, it is difficult to obtain solid CDs because of their hydroscopic nature. With these drawbacks majority of the literature is focused on the application of CDs in solution state and their use in various applications such as optoelectronic devices and sensors that generally require solid state emission of fluorophore, is greatly limited [9]. Several efforts were made to overcome the drawback of ACQ effect of CDs [11–13]. The common strategy involved in these methods is to disperse the fluorescent CDs in solid matrices such as polymers (polyvinyl pyrrolidine, polyacrylamide, & polyvinyl alcohol etc.), silica, titania, laponite and starch [14] etc., to prevent the fluorescence from quenching. However, this dispersion process does not really conquer self-quenching resulted from aggregation; it just prevents the CDs from aggregation. Moreover, this process adds an extra step to the synthesis process making it complex. Hence, there is an urgent need to develop a new method for the direct production of solid state emitting CDs powder.

Furthermore, CDs comprise a large amount of oxygen bearing functional groups which makes them “water loving”. Although this hydrophilic nature is considered to be advantageous for applications like metal ions detection, their poor solubility in other organic solvents significantly hinder their applicability in various fields. Hydrophobic nature is required for various industrial applications such as self-cleaning behaviour, suppression of chemical reactions on the surface and water resistance [15]. Particularly hydrophobic fluorescent probe is extremely useful in detecting analytes in lipophilic media and surfaces [16]. As the cell membranes are chiefly composed of phospholipids, hydrophobic species can be easily uptake by the cell compared to hydrophilic ones [17]. With their inherent properties like high biocompatibility and stable fluorescence combined with hydrophobic nature, the hydrophobic CDs could serve as future alternative for further study in bioimaging [17]. Furthermore, hydrophobic fluorescent probe could be applicable in staining hydrophobic microdomains with resistance to water. Thus the production of organophilic CDs is of great interest to further extend the applicability of CDs to various fields.

Fingerprints analysis has wide application in many fields such as personal identification, access control and is corner stone in the crime scene investigation [18]. Fingerprints are the marks left by the fingers after their contact with a surface and because of their poor optical contrast, most of these fingerprints are invisible making their detection a challenging task [19]. To enhance the optical contrast of these latent fingerprints (LFPs), various approaches such as cyanoacrylate/iodine fuming, vacuum metal deposition, ninhydrin spraying and powder dusting were developed [20]. Among these, powder dusting, being simplest and efficient, became common approach to develop LPFs. The use of fluorescent powders offers high contrast and high sensitivity over conventional white and black powders. Various fluorescent materials including organic dyes, conjugated polymers, and rare earth complexes have been employed for this application [21]. Rapid developments in nanotechnology lead to the usage of wide variety of nanoparticles such as Au nanoparticles, quantum dots and magnetic nanoparticles in the detection of LPFs. However, their cost of preparation, toxicity, short preservation time and destructive nature of detection often hindered their widespread usage. Therefore, developing a rapid, accurate, cost-effective, and no-toxic method for clear visualization and permanent stabilization of LPFs is greatly required.

CDs with their cost effectiveness, low toxicity and fascinating optical properties could serve as an excellent candidate for the visualization of LFPs [22]. However, there are few major hitches which still restrict the application of CDs based nanomaterials for the detection of LFPs. For instance, as mentioned earlier the emission of CDs is drastically quenched by their solid state aggregation which makes it extremely difficult to identify the LFPs by bare eyes. Furthermore, deficiency of surface functional groups that can interact with fingerprint residue in the typical hydrophilic CDs makes it difficult to produce a stable adherence of the CDs on to the LFPs.

In an attempt to address these issues, we have developed a solid state thermal carbonization method of CDs production. This method offered hydrophobic and solid state emitting CDs powder that further explored for the visualization of LFP.
All the chemicals, reagents and solvents used in this study are of analytical grade and no additional purification was applied.

In a typical synthesis, 108 mg of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and 167 mg of 2,6 pyridinedicrboxylic acid (2,6 PDC) (mole ratio, 1:1) were placed in an agate mortar and ground into homogeneous powders. Then the powder was placed in a ceramic crucible, covered with lid and heated in a hot air oven at 180 °C for 4 h. After cooling down to room temperature naturally, the resulted grey colour product is collected by gently scratching the crucible. As obtained NCDs powder exhibited bright green fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp and insoluble in water. Then the NCDs powder was washed with double distilled water to eliminate the unreacted molecules, dried and stored under ambient conditions. For solution studies appropriate amount of NCDs powder was dissolved in ethanol and used.
Complete details on Characterization and Quantum yield calculation are provided in the supporting information.

LFPs were collected from three different individuals on eight different surfaces. To collect the sebum rich fingerprints, fingers from clean hand were gently rubbed across the forehead and stamped onto various surfaces with minimal pressure. All the deposited fingerprints were allowed to age for 24 h at ambient conditions before the development. Conventional powder dusting technique was employed for developing the fingerprints. NCDs powder was carefully applied onto the fingerprints deposited surfaces using a squirrel hair brush. Surplus powder was removed by light brushing action along the ridges of the fingerprints. The developed fingerprints were visualized under long wavelength UV light (365 nm). The fingermark images were consequently observed in UV chamber and captured using canon DSLR camera. All the images are of original representations as photographed and no “Photoshop” was applied.

In the present work, a simple one step approach is reported for the synthesis of solid state emitting hydrophobic CDs. Solid state emitting NCDs powder is obtained by thermal carbonization. Unlike several previous reports, the NCDs did not require any tedious purification process. Water insoluble NCDs powder is dispersed in ethanol for calculating quantum yield (QY). The synthetic conditions were optimized by taking QY as a parameter and the results were tabulated. As shown in Table S1, with increase in the reaction temperature from 150 °C to 180 °C, the QY of formed NCDs increased. However, further increase in temperature to 200 °C and 220 °C resulted in decrease in the QY. Over carbonization at elevated temperatures might be the reason for this decrease in the QY. Reaction time also has similar effect. Increase in the duration of carbonization from 2 h to 4 h caused a clear increase in the QY, however further increase to 5 h resulted a drastic decrease. Here also, over carbonization at prolonged heating might be the reason for the decline in QY. 180 °C temperature and 4 h of reaction time produced NCDs with high QY of 34%.

After optimizing the reaction conditions, the effect of the mole ratio of reactants was also studied. As shown in Table S2, NCDs with high QY were obtained when equal mole ratio of reactants were used. Interestingly, unlike previous reports high amount of amine also decreased the QY. These results suggest that the NCDs were formed by the condensation reaction between the NH2 group of OPD and COOH group of 2,6 PDC which results in the polymerization process and produces polymer chains. The polymer chains further undergo carbonization process to produce carbon dots. The possible formation mechanism of NCDs from OPD and 2,6 PDC is illustrated in Scheme 1 .

The possible formation mechanism can further be supported from the FTIR analysis of reactants and product (Fig. 1 a). FTIR spectra of 2,6 PDC presented a broad peak ranging from 2300 cm−1 to 3200 cm−1 and an intense absorption band at around 1700 cm−1 which corresponds to the characteristic carboxylic acid functional group. OPD presented two small peaks in the range of 3100–3400 cm−1 corresponding to the primary amine group. In comparison to 2,6 PDC, the carbonyl stretching frequency is shifted to shorter wavenumber and the broad O-H/N-H related absorption is shifted to higher energy. These shifts clearly represent the formation of amide bond between the COOH group of 2,6 PDC and primary amine group of OPD. As shown in Scheme 1, the amine group of OPD will react with COOH group of 2,6 PDC to form amide linkage. This process continues to produce polymeric chains, which further undergo carbonization to produce carbon nanoparticles.

Characteristic groups on the exterior of NCDs were probed by FTIR spectroscopy and represented in Fig. 1a. The large broad peak observed at about 3280 cm−1 can be represented as stretching vibration absorption bands of O – H and N – H and the peak at 3057 cm−1 can be assigned to C – H stretching vibrations. Intense peaks at about 1450 cm−1 and 1570 cm−1 can be ascribed to the aromatic C – C stretching vibrations. Peak at 1670 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching modes of C=N/C=O stretching vibration. Further the peaks at 1271 and 1350 cm−1 can be ascribed to the C – O and C – N stretching frequencies respectively. Intense peak at 740 cm−1 can be assigned aromatic C – H bending. Further information regarding the chemical structure of NCDs was accessed from X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The full spectrum shown in Fig. 1b of NCDs exhibited 3 peaks at 284, 399 and 532 eV indicating the presence of three elements i.e. carbon, nitrogen and oxygen respectively. The least intensity for O 1s peak hints the hydrophobic nature. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 1c) is composed of 3 peaks which reveals the presence of carbon in 3 diverse chemical surroundings attributed to C – C/C=C (283.9 eV), C – N/C – O (285.6 eV), and C=O/C=N (287.1 eV) [23]. The N1s peak (Fig. 1d) can be deconvolved into 3 components that designates the existence of N in graphite-like structure (N-C3) (398.0 eV), pyridinic-like Ns (399.6 eV), and N-H bond of NH2 group (401.3 eV) [24]. From FT-IR and XPS analysis, it is clear that the NCDs contain large amount of hydrophobic groups such as aromatic and alkyl groups and only a small amount of hydrophilic groups are observed. These results further support the organophilic nature of the produced NCDs.

The morphology and microstructure of NCDs obtained under optimal conditions were explored by TEM. As displayed in Fig. 2 a, NCDs are spherical in shape, uniform and well distributed without aggregation. The particle size distribution histogram represented in Fig. 2b disclosed their homogeneous size distribution of 2–8 nm and average diameter of 4.59 ± 1.04 nm. Powder XRD patterns of NCDs (shown in Fig. 2c) presented a wide diffraction peak centred at 2 theta equals to 22.4° with an interlayer spacing of 0.37 nm indicating the amorphous carbon structure (poor crystalline nature) [25]. As presented in Fig. 2d, the intensity of amorphous D band is higher than the crystalline G band in the Raman spectra (recorded at 633 nm wavelength), which further confirms the amorphous nature of the NCDs.

UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopy techniques were employed to explore the optical properties of both NCDs powder and liquid dispersion. After synthesis process the obtained NCDs powder appeared as grey coloured under ambient light and exhibited strong blue-green emission under long wavelength (365 nm) UV light. Interestingly similar kind emission with some weak glow is observed under short wavelength (254 nm) UV light. Fig. 3 a shows the excitation and emission spectra of NCDs powder. As revealed from figure, the excitation spectra of NCDs exhibited two peaks centred at 280 nm and 360 nm. These two excitations can be assigned to the core and surface states respectively. The emission spectra presented sharp and intense peak centred at around 485 nm. Interestingly narrow emission peak (FWHM = 60 nm) and very large stoke shift values were observed i.e. 205 nm for the excitation of 280 nm and 125 nm for the excitation of 360 nm. These properties indicate weak self-absorption effect in NCDs powder. Furthermore, the emission spectra is recorded at various excitation wavelengths and depicted in Fig. 3b. As presented in the figure, the emission intensity is altered with increase in the λ ex from 240 nm to 380 nm with an increment of 20 nm. Maximum emission intensity is observed at the excitations of 280 and 360 nm. However unlike several previous reports, the emission peak position did not altered with the change in the excitation wavelength. This might be due to the homogeneous surface nature of formed NCDs [26].

Optical properties of NCDs are further studied by dispersing them in ethanol. The ethanolic solution of NCDs is pale yellow and transparent under ambient light and exhibited bright blue emission under UV light. Fig. 4 a shows absorption, excitation and emission spectra of NCDs dispersed in ethanol. As shown by black line in the figure, the absorption spectrum shows a broad absorption band in the wavelength range of 260 nm–350 nm. This band might correspond to both π – π* electronic transition of core state as well as n – π* transition of heteroatomic surface state and/or molecular state [27]. The excitation spectra presented a peak at 326 nm and the corresponding emission spectra exhibited an intense and narrow (FWHM = 55 nm) peak at around 376 nm with a stoke shift of 50 nm. Fig. 4b shows the emission spectra recorded at increasing excitation wavelengths from 220 nm to 340 nm with an increment of 20 nm. Change in the excitation wavelength caused a change in the emission intensity only, while the peak position remained constant. This kind of excitation wavelength independent emission is observed in solid state also. Major difference in the optical properties of NCDs in the solid and solution state is their emission and excitation peak positions. Compared to solid state the peak positions exhibited a clear blue shift i.e. shift to high energetic side. This shift can be explained based on the well-known fact that the available free energy is high in solution state compared to solid state.

QY of NCDs in ethanol solvent determined by taking quinine sulphate as reference compound is found to be 34.76%. Slope method is employed for this purpose and the corresponding plot of integrated PL intensity vs absorbance is given in Fig. S1. This value of QY is on higher side compared to several previous reports [28–30] and nitrogen doping can be considered as a reason for this high value [31].

It is well known that the emission intensity of several probes will get disrupted by complex surrounding circumstances. Hence to test the ability of NCDs to be used as a fluorescent probe for various practical applications, their fluorescence stability is tested under diverse conditions. Photostability or resistance to photobleaching is an important characteristic for a fluorophore to be used in various applications ranging from imaging to optoelectronics. Photostability studies were performed by continuously irradiating the NCDs sample under 360 nm UV lamp for 1 h. As presented in Fig. 5 a, no significant drop in the emission intensity is witnessed even after 1 h of irradiation indicating the superior photostability of NCDs. Interestingly, NCDs exhibited high photostability in solid state which is advantageous feature as stable solid state emission is key for optoelectronic applications [32]. Furthermore, the shelf life of NCDs is evaluated by keeping them in ambient circumstances. As shown in Fig. 5b, no substantial reduction in the emission intensity is detected even after storing for 60 days and the solution remained clear implying the great stability and long shelf life of NCDs. All these outcomes signify that the produced NCDs are highly stable in both solid state and solution form and can serve as an excellent candidate for diverse applications.

As NCDs exhibited hydrophobic nature, their solubility in various organic solvents is studied. The NCDs powder is soluble in almost all organic solvents (both polar and non-polar) ranging from Methanol, Ethanol, Isopropanol, Chloroform, Ethyl acetate, Acetonitrile, Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), Acetone, Toluene, Dimethyl formamide (DMF) and n-pentane and exhibited bright fluorescence in all solvents.

This feature is highly advantageous and can help in further extending the applications of carbon dots to wide variety of fields. Optical properties of NCDs were studied in four representative organic solvents i.e. methanol, DMSO, ethyl acetate and Toluene. The absorption, excitation and emission spectra of NCDs in these four solvents are represented in Fig. 6 . Furthermore, the parameters like absorption maxima (λ max), excitation peak position (λ ex), emission peak position (λ em), full width at half maxima of emission peak (FWHM) and stoke shift were presented in Table 1 .

When fingers touch a solid surface, the pattern of the ridges and furrows of fingertips get transferred on to the surface and forms fingerprints [33]. As these fingerprints are not visible to naked eyes they are termed as latent finger prints (LFPs) [34]. The LFPs are originated from the secretions of two major glands i.e. eccrine glands and sebaceous glands [35]. Eccrine glands are located only on the palms of hands and soles of feet. The secretions of eccrine glands consists 99% of water and small quantity of inorganic salts such as chlorides, sulphates and phosphates and organic compounds like sugars, amino acids and proteins. Sebaceous glands are associated with hair roots and present all over the body except on the palms of hand and soles of feet. They secrete sebum, which is composed of wax esters, fatty acids and squalene. Thus, the fingerprint residue is mainly composed of eccrine sweat, as well as sebum because of the possible contact of bare hands with face or hair. Moreover, contaminants like cosmetics or other exogenous elements may also be found.

Development of LFPs indeed involves the visualization of informative properties of the papillary ridge patterns. Generally this information can be categorized into three levels [36]. Level 1 presents the details of ridge patterns such as arches, loops, whorls and compounds. Level 2 explains about the minutia points which include short ridge, bifurcation, delta, ridge ending, lake, core, hook, bridge, enclosure, and island, and their random combinations bestow the individuality of the fingerprint. Furthermore, level 3 alludes to the location, number and shape of sweat pores. Even though the level 3 details alone cannot be applied to detect LFPs, they offer reference details for partial defect or blurry level 2 information.

The applicability of NCDs powder for the rapid visualization of LFPs was explored by using powder dusting technique. Before dusting, the NCDs powder is ground to fine powder for improving the efficiency of dusting process. Firstly, LFPs were collected on a surface (microscopic slide) and after aging for 24 h they were developed by dusting with NCDs powder. The developed LFPs were visualized under 365 nm UV lamp. As shown in Fig. 7 , the ridge patterns of LFPs were clearly visualized with bright blue-green emission of NCDs. Analysis of the photographic images of as visualized LFPs clearly presented most of the informative properties of LFPs. As shown in figure, level 1 details i.e. right loop can be easily identified. Furthermore Level 2 details such as short ridge, bifurcation, ridge ending, lake, double bifurcation, hook/spur, core and delta are clearly visible. Moreover, level 3 information (sweat pores) can also be attained. As all these informative properties are highly useful in individual identification, NCDs can be considered as an excellent candidate for rapid visualization of LFPs in forensic analysis and crime scene investigation.

A real crime scene will be more complex in nature and there is a great chance of finding LFPs on various surfaces and backgrounds which come across in our day-to-day life [37]. Hence, as an experiment to evaluate the applicability of NCDs powder in real crime scene investigations, an attempt is made to develop LFPs collected on various surfaces and backgrounds. Various types of objects with porous, semi-porous and non-porous surfaces were selected for this purpose. The backgrounds of these surfaces were chosen as coloured, multi-coloured and auto-fluorescent. The results of this experiment were presented in Fig. 8 . As shown in figure, LFPs detected on various objects including compact disc, glass slide, auto fluorescent lid, magazine paper, leather keychain, floppy disk, aluminium surface and journal cover page are clearly presenting the details of ridge pattern which would provide adequate information for individual identification and these details can be easily identified by bare eyes.

One important observation is that the ridge patterns are clearly visible with good contrast even on an auto-fluorescent background. This can be ascribed to the green emission of NCDs which provides sufficient contrast to the blue auto fluorescent background. This property of emitting green fluorescence under the UV excitation (365 nm) is considered to be superior over several previously reported CDs. As most of the CDs emit strong blue fluorescence under the UV excitation, they are not capable of producing sufficient contrast on an auto-fluorescent background. Though they exhibit excitation dependent emission nature, they require a specialized long wavelength excitation source to produce green/red emission which can create sufficient contrast to the auto fluorescent background [38]. However, no such specialized excitation source is required for NCDs and the LFPs on auto fluorescent background can also be visualized under routine UV lamp (365 nm). Hence, NCDs could be considered as a potential candidate for the rapid visualization LFPs on various surfaces and backgrounds at real crime scene.

In summation, a facile one-step technique for the synthesis of solid state emitting NCDs powder is successfully demonstrated. This method does not require any tedious purification steps and the product is obtained in large quantities. Further the obtained NCDs presented hydrophobic and organophilic nature which is important for industrial applications. Moreover NCDs exhibited highly stable solid state emission with which they can find wide applications in optical and optoelectronic devices. The NCDs powder was explored as a fluorescent developer of LFPs on various porous and non-porous surfaces. With their green emission, they were able to provide sufficient contrast on auto-fluorescent surface also. Furthermore, the NCDs developed LFPs clearly presented level 2 and level 3 ridge details highlighting their potential in crime scene investigation.

Rajkumar Bandi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Harsha gavash Kannikanti: Investigation, Formal analysis, Validation. Ramakrishna dadigala: Validation, Data curation. Bhagavanth reddy Gangapuram: Data curation, Visualization. Jayathirtha rao Vaidya: Resources, Writing - review & editing. Veerabhadram Guttena: Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: One of the authors (RB) gratefully acknowledges CSIR-INDIA for Senior Research Fellowship. HGK would like to thank UGC-New Delhi for the fellowship as UGC-SRF. JRV thanks CSIR-Delhi for Emeritus Scientist honour. The authors are grateful to MRC, MNIT Jaipur for extending various analytical facilities. The authors would like to thank DST-FIST, New Delhi, India for providing necessary analytical facilities in the department.

Acta. 187 (2020) 30. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00604-019-4017-1.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon-quantum-dot-derived nanostructured MnO2 and its symmetrical supercapacitor performances

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Luminescence properties of Eu3+/CDs/PVA composite applied in light conversion film

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesis of highly fl uorescent nitrogen – doped carbon dots from Lantana camara berries for e ff ective detection of lead (II) and bioimaging
